mnemonics: SLL: Static Like a List (fixed size, contiguous) vs Linked Like a Lasso (dynamic, chained)
keyPoints: Static allocation uses a fixed, contiguous block of memory allocated at compile time.
Linked allocation uses non-contiguous memory with nodes connected by pointers, allocated dynamically at runtime.
Static structures allow for direct/index-based access, while linked structures require sequential traversal.
commonMistakes: Assuming linked allocation uses contiguous memory like static arrays.
Attempting to access a static array beyond its pre-allocated fixed size, causing a buffer overflow.
Forgetting to update pointers when inserting/deleting nodes in a linked structure, causing memory leaks or broken links.
